query: differences between choline and amino acids

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acid metabolism may play a critical role in the development of T2DM. Studies have shown that there is a 4-fold increase in isoleucine, phenylalanine, and tyrosine in individuals with hyperglycemia. Researchers found that these amino acids were elevated up to 12 years before the onset of the disease. [56] Recent studies have further elucidated the role of these metabolites in the development, screening, and treatment of metabolic syndrome (MetS), a cardiometabolic cluster that predisposes patients to T2DM and CVD. Studies have shown that choline, L-carnitine, and trimethylamine-N-oxide were associated with inflammatory pathways and increased the risk of metabolic dysfunction in nascent MetS patients, who meet classification for MetS but do not have T2DM and cardiovascular disease. [57] Literature has also shown increased levels of isoleucine and tyrosine and decreased levels of lysine and methionine. These metabolites appear to be early biomarkers of nascent MetS and significant contributors to the pro-inflammatory burden of MetS.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Acetylcholine -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine derives from two constituents, choline, and an acetyl group, the latter derived from the coenzyme acetyl-CoA. [2] Choline is naturally present in foods such as egg yolks, liver, seeds of various vegetables, and legumes. Choline is also produced by the liver natively. Once choline is circulating in the plasma, it can readily cross the blood-brain barrier and be taken up by cholinergic nerve terminals via sodium-dependent uptake channels. [3] [4] The rate-limiting step in acetylcholine production is the availability of acetate derived from mitochondrial acetyl-CoA and choline derived from the plasma directly and from reuptake from the synaptic cleft.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Acetylcholinesterase -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The interaction of acetylcholinesterase with the substrate acetylcholine results in the breakdown, hydrolysis, and inactivation of acetylcholine and subsequent control of the amount of ACh at the synapse. AChE is a serine hydrolase that creates a tetrahedral intermediate through acid-base reactions with a catalytic triad (serine, histidine, acid residue). [8] Histidine allows for the transference of a proton between the oxygen molecules in serine and ACh, thereby removing choline to form a new acylated serine. When the acylated serine is deacylated, the regeneration of free AChE begins. In this reaction, aspartate stabilizes the protonated histidine, which releases acetic acid and a new, free enzyme. The interaction between amino acid residues (tyrosine, phenylalanine, tryptophan) that make up a peripheral anionic site influences the conformational binding of ACh to that site. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudocholinesterase -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pseudocholinesterase is a serine hydrolase enzyme primarily produced in the liver that catalyzes the hydrolysis of choline esters, most prominently succinylcholine and mivacurium. [1] It is crucial to differentiate this enzyme from "true" cholinesterase, also known as acetylcholinesterase. It occurs in higher concentrations within conducting tissues such as the central or peripheral nervous systems and neuromuscular junctions. [2] Due to diverse functions and tissue distribution within the human body, pseudocholinesterase often gets referred to through various enzymatic names, including plasma cholinesterase, serum cholinesterase, acetylcholine acetylhydrolase, and butyrylcholinesterase (BuChE). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Histidine -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Histidine demonstrates diverse roles in cellular function. In addition to playing a structural and catalytic role in many enzymes, histidine residues can undergo enzyme-catalyzed methylation (using S-adenosyl methionine as the methyl donor), as illustrated by the critical role of 3-methylhistidine in the ATP binding site of actin. [7] Histidine residues are also crucial to maintaining the myelin sheath as they participate in the hydroxylation of the galactosylceramide, which is responsible for the compaction of the myelin. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Cholinergic Crisis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Acetyl-CoA is produced from glucose and choline through a reaction catalyzed by çyltransferase (CAT). ACh is then packaged into vesicles at the presynaptic membrane. Each vesicle contains up to 10,000 molecules of ACh. Upon stimulation, calcium ions trigger the release of ACh. The neurotransmitter’s action at the postsynaptic membrane is terminated not by its reuptake but by the action of AChE, a powerful hydrolytic enzyme found in the synaptic cleft. AChE breaks down ACh into choline and acetate. The cholinergic nerve terminal contains a sodium-choline transporter that reabsorbs the choline produced during this hydrolysis process. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Acetylcholine -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Muscarinic acetylcholine receptors are G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) composed of a single polypeptide with seven discrete regions arranged in an α-helix. In these α-helices are hydrophobic residues that allow the polypeptide to span the neuronal cell membrane seven times. The carboxy-tail and fifth cytoplasmic facing loop of this polypeptide are the regions that interact with the second messenger system G proteins. G proteins are composed of an α, β, and γ subunit. When the muscarinic receptor is bound by acetylcholine, it will change its conformation, causing the α subunit to release the natively bound guanosine diphosphate molecule (GDP) and trade it for guanosine triphosphate (GTP). Once GTP binds to the α subunit, the α subunit will dissociate from the β and γ subunits and interact with other downstream effector molecules. The α subunit has intrinsic GTPase activity and will eventually catalyze GTP back into GDP, thus turning the second messenger system “off” intrinsically with time. The use of second messenger pathways to exert their effects allows muscarinic receptors to have diverse functions in human physiology that are tissue-dependent and can be either inhibitory or stimulatory.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Acetylcholine -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Termination of acetylcholine action in the synaptic junction occurs when acetylcholine rapidly binds, then unbinds from its receptor in the target cell’s surface and gets subsequently cleaved by acetylcholinesterase into choline and acetate. Acetylcholinesterase is present in the synaptic cleft as a free molecule or GPI-linked protein on the surface of the postsynaptic cell surface. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholinergic Receptors -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: In the development process of the central nervous system, cholinergic receptors influence neuronal cell growth and survival, cell differentiation, and synapse formation. Nicotinic receptors compose some of the first receptor proteins observed in CNS development. Expression of various nicotinic receptor subtypes in the brain influences cell migration, neuronal outgrowth, and signaling pathways. The varied expression of receptor subtypes can confer different development pathways in the brain. Receptor subtypes may exhibit separate ionic permeability. Receptors that are permeable to Ca++ exhibit a regional variance in activating second messenger systems, stimulating the growth of neuronal progenitor cells or the activation of gene expression through indirect phosphorylation of the cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) response element-binding protein (CREB).

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Amino Acid Synthesis and Degradation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Methionine is converted to S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) by methionine adenosyltransferase. Loss of methionine has correlated with an accumulation of hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2 ) in hair follicles, a decrease in tyrosinase effectiveness, and a gradual loss of the natural hair color. [14] Methionine is crucial for the increase in the intracellular concentration of glutathione (GSH). GSH is an antioxidant found in animals, plants, fungi, bacteria, and archaea. Promoting antioxidant-mediated cell defense and redox regulation is critical in protecting cells against dopamine-induced nigral cell loss by oxidative binding metabolites. [15] Methionine is an amino acid, an intermediate component for the biosynthesis of some amino acids. These amino acids are cysteine, carnitine, taurine, lecithin, and phosphatidylcholine. Methionine is also a medium used in the biosynthesis of additional phospholipids. Improper transformation of methionine can lead to atherosclerosis due to the accumulation of homocysteine. Moreover, this amino acid is essential to reversing the damaging methylation of the glucocorticoid receptor gene caused by repeated stress exposures, with implications for depression. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Cholinesterase Inhibitors -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Cholinesterase inhibitors function by inhibiting cholinesterase from hydrolyzing acetylcholine into its components of acetate and choline'; this allows for an increase in the availability and duration of action of acetylcholine in neuromuscular junctions. The cholinesterase enzyme has two active sites: an anionic site formed by tryptophan and an esteractic site formed by serine. Cholinesterase inhibitors such as organophosphates inhibit cholinesterase from cleaving acetylcholine by interacting with the serine esteractic site. As a result, acetylcholine will continue to accumulate and activate associated receptors. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Acetylcholinesterase -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with Alzheimer disease often receive treatment with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors that mitigate symptoms by hindering ACh turnover. In effect, lingering levels of ACh help recalibrate the neurotransmitter to appropriate and adequate levels. [5] Inhibition of AChE raises the concentration of ACh at the synaptic junction and allows for the potentiation of the signal. This action ultimately reduces the amount of choline uptake and increases the number of muscarinic M2 receptors. In those treated with AChE inhibitors, a deceleration of the progression of the disease, as well as an increased attention span, has been reported. However, no significant signs of increased short-term memory have been noted in the current literature. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 680
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids are presented as a central biochemical family that underlies neurotransmission, drug action, and metabolic regulation. Within the broader “Amino acids and derivatives” class, compounds such as L‑carnitine, S‑adenosylmethionine (SAM), betaine, glutamine, and the peptide hormone metreleptin are listed. Amino acids are also members of the “Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators” group, serving as precursors or modulators of neurotransmitter activity. SAM and its precursor S‑adenosylhomocysteine are child concepts of amino acids; SAM further generates adenosine, which is itself classified as a neurotransmitter. Additional specialized derivatives include chloromethylketony amino acids, dihydroxyphenylalanine (the precursor of dopamine), levodopa (L‑DOPA), and other narrow‑relationship compounds that connect amino‑acid chemistry to dopamine synthesis. Glutamine is linked hierarchically to the drug class proglumid and to the investigational agent pomaglumetad methionil, illustrating its relevance in pharmacology; proline is likewise a parent concept within the amino‑acid hierarchy. Dopaminergic agents are defined as a subclass of neurotransmitters and include specific chemical entities such as 1‑methyl‑4‑fenyl‑1,2,3,6‑tetrahydropyridine and apomorphine, the latter being a morphine‑derived compound that stimulates dopamine receptors. Acetylcholine, another member of the Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators subcluster, is derived from choline and exists in a narrower salt form, acetylcholine chloride. The network emphasizes that amino acids and their derivatives provide the structural foundation for endogenous neurotransmitters (e.g., acetylcholine, adenosine, dopamine) and for pharmacologic agents (e.g., L‑DOPA, levodopa, apomorphine, proglumid), highlighting their biosynthetic links, hierarchical classifications, and therapeutic relevance in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 567
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids are organic, nitrogen‑containing carboxylic acids that serve as fundamental protein building blocks and as precursors for several neurotransmitters and neuroregulators, including dopamine and acetylcholine. Representative members of the amino‑acid family include proline (a prototypical parent), glycine, levodopa (L‑DOPA), chloromethyl‑ketone amino acids, and S‑adenosyl‑L‑homocysteine, each highlighting specialized biochemical roles. Because of their precursor function, amino acids are explicitly linked to the “Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators” subcluster and to neurotransmitter synthesis pathways in general. Acetylcholine exemplifies the overlap between chemical classification and function: it is a nitrogen‑containing compound, a neurotransmitter and neuroregulator, and the active moiety of the ophthalmic preparation acetylcholine chloride 10 mg/mL (Miochol). Adenosine is also classified as a neurotransmitter and is derived from S‑adenosyl‑L‑homocysteine. Dopaminergic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1152
retrieved_docs.content: The compound 1‑Methyl‑4‑fenyl‑1,2,3,6‑tetrahydropyridin is classified as a dopaminergic agent that acts at dopamine receptors. L‑carnitine, S‑adenosyl‑L‑methionine (SAMe), betaine and glutamine are members of the broader “amino acids and derivatives” class, and within this hierarchy S‑adenosyl‑homocysteine (SAH) and SAMe are child entities; SAMe further contains adenosine as a subordinate compound. Specific amino‑acid derivatives such as chloromethyl‑ketone amino acids, dihydroxyphenylalanine (L‑DOPA) and levodopa are treated as narrower entities under the amino‑acid umbrella, reflecting their role as specialized precursors of catecholamine neurotransmitters. Glutamine, proline and 6‑aminocaproic acid are identified as individual amino‑acid members (parents) within the family. The “Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators” category explicitly includes amino acids, acetylcholine and dopaminergic agents, underscoring the fundamental link between amino‑acid metabolism and neurotransmitter synthesis. Adenosine, acetylcholine and dopaminergic agents are all classified as neurotransmitters; SAH serves as a metabolic precursor of adenosine, making adenosine a downstream product. Dopaminergic agents belong to the broader neurotransmitter class and encompass mechanisms such as inhibitory dopamine reuptake, with L‑DOPA highlighted as a specific dopaminergic medication. Additionally, the amino‑acid class is linked to the compound pomaglumetad‑methionil through a mapping relationship, indicating a pharmacological association. Collectively, these relationships depict a network in which amino acids and their derivatives form the structural foundation for neurotransmitter agents, dopaminergic drugs, and related therapeutic compounds, emphasizing their central importance in neurochemical function and drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 314
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids and derivatives comprise several biologically important compounds, including L‑carnitine, S‑adenosylmethionine, and glutamine, each of which is classified as a member of this chemical group. These molecules play key roles in cellular metabolism, energy production, methyl‑group transfer, and nitrogen balance, making them essential for normal physiological function and for the management of various metabolic disorders. In contrast, metreleptin, a recombinant leptin analog used to treat leptin deficiency and related metabolic conditions, does not belong to the amino acids and derivatives category; it is a protein‑based therapeutic rather than an amino‑acid‑derived compound. Recognizing the correct classification of these entities supports accurate understanding of their mechanisms of action and appropriate clinical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionine (S‑adenosylmethionin) and glutamine are both classified as members of the broader chemical group “amino acids and derivatives,” indicating that they share the fundamental structural features of amino acids and related compounds. This categorization reflects their roles in metabolism and biochemistry, as both participate in key physiological pathways typical of amino‑acid‑derived molecules. Recognizing S‑adenosylmethionine and glutamine as amino‑acid derivatives helps clinicians and researchers understand their functions, interactions, and potential therapeutic relevance within the context of amino‑acid metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 458
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosyl‑L‑methionine and S‑adenosyl‑L‑homocysteine are closely linked to the nucleoside adenosine, a neurotransmitter, representing downstream products of adenosine metabolism; glutamine is a downstream component of the drug proglumid. Acetylcholine (acetylcholin) is a member of the “Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators” category, functions as a neurotransmitter alongside adenosine, and serves as the active moiety of the ophthalmic preparation acetylcholine chloride 10 mg/mL (Miochol). Dopaminergic agents are a subclass of neurotransmitter substances and include dopamine‑reuptake inhibitors as well as levodopa, carbidopa, and the precursor dihydroxyphenylalanine (L‑DOPA). Collectively, these relationships integrate basic biochemical building blocks, metabolic intermediates, and therapeutic agents within the functional domains of proteins, neurotransmitters, and drug classes, highlighting the central importance of amino acids in metabolic pathways and neuropharmacology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1557
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionin (S‑adenosylmethionine) and betain are both classified as members of the broader biochemical group “Amino acids and derivatives.” In addition, the therapeutic protein metreleptin is also considered a member of this “Amino acids and derivatives” category, reflecting its composition of amino‑acid residues. Recognizing that S‑adenosylmethionin, betain, and metreleptin share this common classification highlights their related metabolic origins and can inform their roles in physiological processes and potential therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 426
retrieved_docs.content: L‑carnitine, S‑adenosylmethionine, glutamine and metreleptin are all classified as members of the “Amino acids and derivatives” group, indicating that each of these compounds is considered an amino‑acid‑related molecule or derivative. In addition, each of these entities is listed within the NCI Drug Dictionary Terminology, meaning they are recognized drug‑related concepts in that curated pharmacologic resource. This dual classification highlights the metabolic and therapeutic relevance of these amino‑acid‑derived substances and supports their inclusion in both biochemical and drug‑information contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1722
retrieved_docs.content: L‑carnitine (L‑karnitin) and S‑adenosylmethionine (S‑adenosylmethionin) are both classified as members of the “Amino acids and derivatives” group, a category that encompasses compounds derived from amino acids and involved in numerous metabolic pathways. By belonging to this class, both L‑carnitine and S‑adenosylmethionine share structural and functional characteristics typical of amino‑acid‑derived molecules, highlighting their roles in cellular metabolism and biochemical regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1902
retrieved_docs.content: L‑karnitin, S‑adenosylmethionin, and glutamin are all classified as members of the broader category “Amino acids and derivatives.” This grouping includes both standard amino acids and related compounds that serve essential roles in metabolism, such as energy transport (L‑karnitin), methyl group donation (S‑adenosylmethionin), and nitrogen balance and protein synthesis (glutamin). Recognizing that these three substances belong to the Amino acids and derivatives class helps clinicians and researchers appreciate their shared biochemical context and informs their use in therapeutic and nutritional strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids and derivatives constitute a biochemical class that includes several important metabolites such as S‑adenosylmethionin (commonly known as S‑adenosylmethionine), betaine, and glutamin (glutamine). These compounds are directly derived from or closely related to amino acids and serve distinct physiological functions: S‑adenosylmethionin acts as a universal methyl‑group donor in numerous methylation reactions; betaine functions as an osmolyte and methyl donor that helps protect cells from osmotic stress; and glutamin is a key nitrogen carrier that supports protein synthesis, energy production, and acid‑base balance. Although some sources may erroneously list metreleptin as a member of this group, metreleptin is a recombinant leptin analog and does not belong to the amino‑acid‑derived compound class. Correctly distinguishing which substances are true members of the amino acids and derivatives category is essential for accurate biochemical interpretation, therapeutic decision‑making, and patient education.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: Neuroregulators subcluster encompasses both amino acids and acetylcholine, reflecting the interdisciplinary nature of these molecules in neuropharmacology and psychology. Additionally, the supplement N.O.-Xplode is mapped to the amino‑acid category, suggesting it contains or is related to amino‑acid components. Collectively, this network illustrates how amino‑acid derivatives serve as precursors or direct agents for major neurotransmitter systems, including dopaminergic and cholinergic pathways, and how various pharmacological agents (e.g., dopaminergic drugs, acetylcholine salts) are organized within these interconnected hierarchies, informing both biochemical understanding and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 322
retrieved_docs.content: The compound 1‑methyl‑4‑fenyl‑1,2,3,6‑tetrahydropyridin is classified as a dopaminergic agent that modulates dopamine neurotransmission. Aminokyseliny denotes a subclass of amino acids, peptides, or proteins and includes L‑karnitin, S‑adenosylmethionin, glutamin, prolin, dihydroxyfenylalanin (L‑DOPA), levodopa, chloromethylketony aminokyselin, and related derivatives. Both L‑karnitin and S‑adenosylmethionin are also listed under the broader category “Amino acids and derivatives.” S‑adenosylmethionin contains adenosine as a child entity; adenosine is linked to the neurotransmitter family. S‑adenosylhomocysteine is metabolically connected to adenosine, serving as a precursor that yields adenosine through hydrolysis, and is related to aminokyseliny as a parent concept in the metabolic pathway of these compounds. Acetylcholine, derived from its parent molecule choline, is classified as a neurotransmitter and is included in the Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators group together with aminokyseliny and acetylcholin. Levodopa and dihydroxyfenylalanin function as dopaminergic agents used clinically to augment dopamine levels in Parkinson’s disease. The network also connects pomaglumetad methionil to aminokyseliny, and Metreleptin (a peptide hormone) to the amino‑acid‑derived classification. Overall, the relationships illustrate how amino‑acid‑derived molecules such as levodopa, dihydroxyfenylalanin, adenosine, and acetylcholine serve as neurotransmitters or precursors, how S‑adenosylmethionin and S‑adenosylhomocysteine participate in their metabolism, and how pharmacologic agents like 1‑methyl‑4‑fenyl‑1,2,3,6‑tetrahydropyridin target dopaminergic systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1561
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholin is identified as a neurotransmitery, indicating its role as a chemical messenger in the nervous system. It is biochemically derived from cholin, which serves as its parent compound, highlighting the metabolic pathway by which acetylcholin is synthesized from choline precursors. This connection underscores the importance of choline availability for proper cholinergic neurotransmission.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1165
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids and derivatives is a biochemical category that encompasses several specific compounds, including L‑carnitine, S‑adenosylmethionine, and glutamine. These three substances share the common characteristic of being either amino acids themselves or closely related metabolic derivatives. In contrast, metreleptin—a recombinant leptin analog used in the treatment of leptin deficiency—is not a member of the amino acids and derivatives class, reflecting its distinct protein‑based structure and therapeutic role. Additionally, glutamine is correctly classified as an amino acid rather than being derived from or having a parent relationship with proglumide; proglumide is an unrelated cholecystokinin‑antagonist drug. Recognizing the proper classification of these molecules supports accurate biochemical understanding and appropriate clinical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 265
retrieved_docs.content: in which amino acids serve as foundational molecules that give rise to or interact with key neurotransmitters such as dopamine, acetylcholine, and adenosine, and from which therapeutic agents like levodopa, karbidopa, and investigational compounds are derived. Understanding these interconnections supports a comprehensive view of neurochemical function and informs the development and use of neuropharmacologic treatments.
